title: Tree traversal cursor and mutation operations
purpose: Deliver the traversal and mutation layer of the prolly tree. CORE-001 established the node types, hashing, and node store. This brief builds on those foundations to provide a cursor that navigates the tree for point lookups and range scans, and a mutation module that inserts and deletes key-value pairs while maintaining content-addressed invariants — splitting nodes at rolling hash boundaries, merging undersized nodes after deletion, and rewriting the path to root so every modified node receives a new hash while unmodified subtrees retain their existing hashes. These operations are the read and write core that the WAL buffer (CORE-005) and shard actor (CORE-007) will call directly.
task: Create two files: crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs (tree traversal cursor for point lookup and range iteration) and crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs (insert, delete, node split/merge, path-to-root rewrite, batch mutation). Wire both into tree/mod.rs. The cursor and mutation functions operate against the NodeStore trait from CORE-001 — they do not open disk storage, manage WAL buffers, or know about shards. Those concerns belong to CORE-004, CORE-005, and CORE-007 respectively. In addition to single-key insert/delete, this brief provides batch_mutate: a function that takes a sorted set of mutations (puts and deletes) and applies them in a single tree walk, producing one root hash. This is what CORE-005's WAL commit calls to satisfy CN8 (one path-to-root rewrite per commit).

requirements.title: Cursor type for tree traversal
requirements.spec: Define a Cursor struct that holds a reference to a NodeStore and a root Hash. THE SYSTEM SHALL initialise a Cursor via Cursor::new(store, root_hash). THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT allocate the full path to root at construction time — path state is accumulated lazily during traversal. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT hold a mutable borrow on the store — the cursor is a read-only view.
acceptance: Cursor::new(store, root_hash) compiles where store: &impl NodeStore
Cursor holds the root hash and a store reference, not a cloned store
Cursor does not mutate the store — it takes a shared reference
Cursor implements Debug
Cursor<'_> compiles with an explicit lifetime on the store reference

files: crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:1-1 — existing CORE-002 placeholder; implement `Cursor` and `TreeError` here rather than adding logic to module roots.
crates/haematite/src/store/memory.rs:6-10 — `NodeStore` requires `Debug`, has shared `get`, and mutable `put`; `Cursor` should hold only a shared store reference and use only `get`.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:9-24 — `Hash` is a `Copy` 32-byte newtype with byte helpers, so the cursor can store the root hash by value.
crates/haematite/Cargo.toml:18-24 — clippy denies `unwrap`, `expect`, and `panic`; even constructor/traversal helpers must stay panic-free.
context: The brief says to create `cursor.rs`, but the file already exists as a one-line placeholder; treat it as the target implementation file.
Use a generic/lifetime-bearing cursor shape such as `Cursor<'a, S: NodeStore + ?Sized> { store: &'a S, root_hash: Hash }`; deriving `Debug` is compatible because `NodeStore: Debug`.
`Cursor::new` should be a cheap initializer: assign `store` and `root_hash` only, and do not fetch the root or allocate a path stack until traversal methods run.
There are no public `Key`/`Value` aliases yet, so match existing `LeafNode` conventions: lookup bounds are byte slices and returned values are owned `Vec<u8>` clones.
scout.approach: Define `#[derive(Debug)] pub struct Cursor<'a, S: NodeStore + ?Sized> { store: &'a S, root_hash: Hash }` in `cursor.rs`, with `pub const fn new(store: &'a S, root_hash: Hash) -> Self` if const constraints permit, otherwise a normal `fn`. Keep fields private to prevent callers from depending on traversal internals; add small accessors only if tests or downstream code need them. The constructor should compile for `let store: &impl NodeStore = ...; Cursor::new(store, root_hash)` and for an explicitly typed `Cursor<'_, _>` without cloning or mutably borrowing the store.
scout.notes: Do not store `MemoryStore` or `Box<dyn NodeStore>` in the cursor; that would either clone/own the store or complicate lifetimes. `S: ?Sized` is useful if later callers pass `&dyn NodeStore`.

files_changed.note: Replaced the CORE-002 placeholder with Cursor, TreeError, traversal helpers, and lazy range iterator support.

dev.how: Added `#[derive(Debug)] pub struct Cursor<'a, S: NodeStore + ?Sized = dyn NodeStore + 'a>` storing only `&'a S` and a by-value `Hash`. `Cursor::new` is a cheap `const fn` that does not fetch nodes or allocate traversal state, and the cursor uses only the shared `NodeStore::get` path for read-only traversal.
dev.deviation: Added a small `root_hash()` read-only accessor and a default trait-object store parameter so both `Cursor<'_, _>` and `Cursor<'_>` compile cleanly; otherwise followed the scouted generic/lifetime-bearing cursor shape.

checklist.note: Cursor now provides `get(key)` traversal support returning `Result<Option<Vec<u8>>, TreeError>`.

acceptance.criterion: Cursor::new(store, root_hash) compiles where store: &impl NodeStore
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:37-40 defines `Cursor::new(store: &'a S, root_hash: Hash)`. Acceptance test `cursor_is_shared_lazy_and_debuggable` compiles `Cursor::new(&store, root)` at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:139.

acceptance.criterion: Cursor holds the root hash and a store reference, not a cloned store
acceptance.evidence: crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:31-35 stores `store: &'a S` and `root_hash: Hash`; the constructor only assigns those fields at cursor.rs:38-40. Test verifies no store reads at construction at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:135-146.

acceptance.criterion: Cursor does not mutate the store — it takes a shared reference
acceptance.evidence: `Cursor::new` takes `&'a S` at crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:38, and `get` uses shared `load_node(self.store, ...)` at cursor.rs:50; no mutable store reference is held by Cursor.

acceptance.criterion: Cursor implements Debug
acceptance.evidence: `#[derive(Debug)]` is on Cursor at crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:31; the hardening test formats it at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:146.

acceptance.criterion: Cursor<'_> compiles with an explicit lifetime on the store reference
acceptance.evidence: Hardening test compiles `let cursor: Cursor<'_, CountingStore> = ...` and trait-object default `let object_cursor: Cursor<'_> = ...` at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:139-141.

fixes: Added acceptance coverage in crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs::cursor_is_shared_lazy_and_debuggable for shared/lazy construction, Debug, and explicit lifetime forms.

requirements.title: Point lookup via cursor
requirements.spec: WHEN get(key) is called on a Cursor, THE SYSTEM SHALL traverse the tree from root to the appropriate leaf by comparing keys against InternalNode child separators at each level, then search the LeafNode entries for an exact key match and return Some(value) or None. IF any node hash in the traversal path is missing from the store, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL return a TreeError::MissingNode error. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT load sibling nodes during a point lookup — only the nodes on the key's direct path are fetched.
acceptance: Cursor::get(key) returns Ok(Some(value)) when the key exists in the leaf
Cursor::get(key) returns Ok(None) when the key is absent from the tree
Cursor::get(key) returns Err(TreeError::MissingNode) when a node hash is not in the store
get on a tree of depth 3 fetches exactly 3 nodes (root + one internal + one leaf)
get on an empty tree (empty root LeafNode) returns Ok(None)

files: crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:1-1 — add `Cursor::get` and traversal helpers here.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:62-75 — `LeafNode` owns private sorted entries and exposes `entries()` for exact-key lookup.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:107-120 — `InternalNode` stores sorted `(separator_key, child_hash)` pairs used to choose the next child.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:152-181 — `Node` enum variants and delegated `hash`/serialization methods define the values returned by the store.
crates/haematite/src/store/memory.rs:6-10 — `NodeStore::get(&Hash) -> Option<Node>` is the only read API; `None` must become `TreeError::MissingNode`.
context: Treat internal node keys as child separators, conventionally the first key in each child. For point lookup, choose the greatest separator `<= key`, falling back to child 0 for keys below the first separator; an empty internal node should be `TreeError::InvalidNode`.
A point lookup should call `store.get` exactly once per visited node hash. Do not pre-load sibling nodes or scan adjacent branches to prove absence.
Use the sorted leaf slice for exact lookup (`binary_search_by` or equivalent) and clone only the matched value for `Ok(Some(value))`.

The current `NodeStore` API cannot distinguish missing storage from deserialization failure because it returns `Option<Node>`; for cursor traversal, `None` should be reported as `MissingNode { hash }`.
scout.approach: Implement `pub fn get(&self, key: &[u8]) -> Result<Option<Vec<u8>>, TreeError>` as an iterative root-to-leaf loop. Start from `self.root_hash`, fetch the node with a helper like `load_node(hash)`, map `None` to `MissingNode`, and match on `Node`: for `Leaf`, binary-search `leaf.entries()` for `key`; for `Internal`, compute the single child index from sorted separators and continue with that child hash. Keep the helper pure and local to cursor traversal so it can be reused by range iteration without introducing mutation or sibling fetches.
scout.notes: Tests for “depth 3 fetches exactly 3 nodes” will likely wrap `NodeStore` with a counter; avoid helper patterns that fetch the same hash twice for validation or logging. Also decide and document the child-separator fallback for keys below the first separator, because absent-low-key tests will exercise it.

files_changed.note: Added point lookup traversal and shared node-loading/child-selection helpers.

dev.how: Implemented `Cursor::get(&self, key: &[u8]) -> Result<Option<Vec<u8>>, TreeError>` as an iterative root-to-leaf walk. It fetches exactly the current path node, chooses the greatest separator `<= key` with child-0 fallback, binary-searches leaf entries, clones only the matched value, and maps missing store lookups to `TreeError::MissingNode { hash }`.

checklist.note: Point lookup traverses internal separators to the target leaf and returns the matching value or `None`.

stories.note: The cursor lookup API exposes transparent key/value retrieval independent of any shard ownership details.

acceptance.criterion: Cursor::get(key) returns Ok(Some(value)) when the key exists in the leaf
acceptance.evidence: `Cursor::get` descends and calls `leaf_value` at crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:46-62 and cursor.rs:248-260. Test `cursor_get_follows_only_the_direct_path` asserts `Some(b"two")` at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:164-166.

acceptance.criterion: Cursor::get(key) returns Ok(None) when the key is absent from the tree
acceptance.evidence: `leaf_value` maps binary-search miss to `Ok(None)` at crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:248-260. Test asserts absent key `b"n"` returns None at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:164-167.

acceptance.criterion: Cursor::get(key) returns Err(TreeError::MissingNode) when a node hash is not in the store
acceptance.evidence: `load_node` maps missing store lookup to `TreeError::MissingNode { hash }` at crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:231-233. Test asserts the exact missing hash at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:172-180.

acceptance.criterion: get on a tree of depth 3 fetches exactly 3 nodes (root + one internal + one leaf)
acceptance.evidence: CountingStore increments reads on get at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:37-46; `cursor_get_follows_only_the_direct_path` builds root/internal/leaf depth and asserts `read_count() == 3` at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:150-167.

acceptance.criterion: get on an empty tree (empty root LeafNode) returns Ok(None)
acceptance.evidence: `cursor_get_reports_missing_nodes_and_empty_leaf_absence` creates an empty root and asserts `None` at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:182-184.

fixes: Added direct-path read counting, missing-node, and empty-leaf get coverage in crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs.

requirements.title: Range scan via cursor
requirements.spec: WHEN range(from, to) is called on a Cursor, THE SYSTEM SHALL return an iterator that yields (key, value) pairs in ascending key order for all keys K satisfying from <= K < to. THE SYSTEM SHALL descend to the leftmost leaf in range at the first call to next(), then advance through leaves by following the implicit sorted order of the prolly tree. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT buffer all results in memory before returning the first item — the iterator must be lazy. IF to is less than or equal to from, THE SYSTEM SHALL return an empty iterator without error.
acceptance: range(b"a", b"z") returns all keys in [a, z) in ascending order
range(b"a", b"a") returns an empty iterator
range(b"z", b"a") returns an empty iterator
range with from and to spanning a leaf boundary returns entries from both leaves in sorted order
The iterator yields the first item without loading all matching leaves first (lazy descent)
range(b"", b"\xff") returns every key-value pair in the tree in sorted order

files: crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:1-1 — implement `range` plus a lazy iterator type here.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:73-75 — leaf entries are already exposed in sorted key order for in-leaf scanning.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:118-120 — internal children are exposed in sorted separator order for stack-based next-leaf traversal.
crates/haematite/src/store/memory.rs:6-10 — the iterator must use the same read-only `NodeStore::get` API as point lookup.
docs/design/core/briefs/CORE-002.json:52-62 — acceptance requires half-open bounds, leaf-boundary spanning, and first-item laziness.
context: There are no leaf sibling pointers, so “advance through leaves by implicit sorted order” means the iterator must keep an owned stack of internal-node child lists and child indexes while descending and ascending.

Because missing-node errors can occur lazily after `range()` returns, prefer an iterator item type that can propagate errors, e.g. `Iterator<Item = Result<(Vec<u8>, Vec<u8>), TreeError>>`; tests can collect into `Result<Vec<_>, _>`.
If `to <= from`, return an already-exhausted iterator without fetching the root. This is both required by acceptance and an easy way to prove no work is done for empty ranges.
The acceptance example `range(b"", b"\xff")` assumes ordinary test keys below `0xff`; do not special-case it as an “all keys” sentinel if arbitrary binary keys are later allowed.
scout.approach: Add `pub fn range(&self, from: &[u8], to: &[u8]) -> RangeIter<'_, S>` (or a `Result<RangeIter, TreeError>` if you choose to load immediately, though the spec prefers first `next()` descent). `RangeIter` should copy `from`/`to` into `Vec<u8>`, defer its initial descent until the first `next()`, choose the leftmost target leaf using the same separator algorithm as `get`, set the leaf entry index to the first key `>= from`, and yield entries until key `>= to`. When a leaf is exhausted, pop stack frames until a frame has a next child, descend to that child’s leftmost leaf, and continue; never collect all matching entries or leaves before returning the first item.
scout.notes: The lazy iterator will own cloned child vectors from internal nodes because `NodeStore::get` returns owned `Node`s. That is acceptable; the key requirement is not buffering all result entries or all matching leaves.

files_changed.note: Added `Cursor::range` and `RangeIter` with lazy stack-based traversal.

dev.how: Implemented `range(from, to)` returning a lazy `RangeIter` whose items are `Result<(Vec<u8>, Vec<u8>), TreeError>`. Empty or reversed bounds finish without reading the root; otherwise the first `next()` descends to the leftmost relevant leaf, scans entries in ascending order, and advances across leaf boundaries by keeping owned internal child/index stack frames rather than buffering all results.

checklist.note: Range iteration yields half-open `[from, to)` key/value pairs in sorted order and can propagate lazy traversal errors.

stories.note: Application range scans now return sequential data in sorted key order.

acceptance.criterion: range(b"a", b"z") returns all keys in [a, z) in ascending order
acceptance.evidence: Range iterator yields ordered items from the stack traversal at crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:179-222. Test `range_is_sorted_half_open_and_lazy_across_leaves` asserts a, m, x in order at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:200-216.

acceptance.criterion: range(b"a", b"a") returns an empty iterator
acceptance.evidence: RangeIter marks `finished: to <= from` at crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:82-94. Test asserts empty at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:227.

acceptance.criterion: range(b"z", b"a") returns an empty iterator
acceptance.evidence: Same empty/reversed bound guard at crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:82-94; test asserts empty at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:228.

acceptance.criterion: range with from and to spanning a leaf boundary returns entries from both leaves in sorted order
acceptance.evidence: The test constructs three leaves under one root at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:190-197 and then asserts items from separate leaves are yielded at lines 203-216.

acceptance.criterion: The iterator yields the first item without loading all matching leaves first (lazy descent)
acceptance.evidence: `RangeIter::new` only stores bounds and does not call `load_node` at crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:82-95; `next` starts traversal lazily at cursor.rs:187-195. Test asserts 0 reads before next and only 2 reads after first item at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:200-207.

acceptance.criterion: range(b"", b"\xff") returns every key-value pair in the tree in sorted order
acceptance.evidence: Hardening test collects `range(b"", b"\xff")` and asserts all key/value pairs in order at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:218-226.

fixes: Added range acceptance coverage for half-open bounds, reversed/empty bounds, leaf-boundary advancement, full scan, and laziness.

requirements.title: Insert with node splitting and path-to-root rewrite
requirements.spec: Define an insert(store, root_hash, key, value) function in the mutate module. WHEN insert is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL traverse to the target leaf, insert the key-value pair in sorted position, apply rolling hash boundary detection across the leaf's key stream, and split the leaf into two or more nodes wherever a boundary falls. THE SYSTEM SHALL propagate any new child separators up through internal nodes, splitting internal nodes that exceed their boundary as well, until the path to root is rewritten with new hashes for every modified node. THE SYSTEM SHALL return the new root Hash. IF the root itself splits, THE SYSTEM SHALL create a new InternalNode with two children and return its hash as the new root. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT modify node objects already in the store — every modified node is a new node with a new hash (P1). THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT alter unmodified sibling subtrees — their existing hashes propagate unchanged into the rewritten internal nodes (P4, C18).
acceptance: insert(store, root_hash, key, value) returns a new root Hash distinct from the old one
A Cursor on the new root can get(key) and returns the inserted value
Inserting the same key twice returns a new root where get(key) returns the second value
When a leaf split occurs, both resulting leaves are stored in the store under their new hashes
When a root split occurs, the returned hash points to a new InternalNode with exactly two children
Unmodified sibling subtrees appear in the new InternalNode with their original child hashes unchanged
Inserting keys in different orders into an empty tree produces the same root hash (history-independence, CN5, C17)

files: crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:1-1 — existing placeholder for `insert` and shared mutation helpers.
crates/haematite/src/tree/boundary.rs:1-49 — `BoundaryDetector::default()` is a deterministic 4 KiB target splitter using BLAKE3(key) modulo target size.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:62-150 — `LeafNode`/`InternalNode` constructors enforce sorted unique keys and expose entries/children for rebuilding modified nodes.
crates/haematite/src/store/memory.rs:6-10 — mutation functions need `&mut impl NodeStore` because only `put(&mut self, &Node) -> Hash` writes new content-addressed nodes.
docs/design/core/design.json:7-13 — P1/P4 require content identity and unchanged subtree reuse, not in-place edits or full-tree rewrites.
context: Build new nodes rather than mutating stored ones: clone leaf/internal contents into local vectors, construct new `LeafNode`/`InternalNode`, store them with `put`, and propagate returned hashes upward.

Boundary detection is pure per key, but the split input must be the modified node’s sorted key stream. If split placement depends on insertion order or old leaf size, CN5 history independence will fail.
Parent child separators need to track the first key in each child. Inserting a new smallest key into a child must update that child’s separator in the rewritten parent even if no split occurs.
A full flatten-and-rebuild implementation would be simpler and history-independent, but it would violate C18/P4 and the acceptance criterion that unmodified sibling hashes survive unchanged.
scout.approach: Implement insertion through shared mutation machinery: traverse from root to the target leaf, recording path frames `(internal_children, selected_index)`, insert or replace the key in sorted position, split the resulting leaf entries at deterministic boundary positions, store each new leaf, and bubble a `Vec<(separator, hash)>` of replacement child refs back up. At each parent, splice the replacement child refs into the cloned child vector, update separators, split internal children with the same boundary detector over child separator keys, store new internal nodes, and continue to the root. If bubbling produces multiple root-level child refs, create a new `InternalNode` root from them; tests for a single leaf/root split should see exactly two children when the inserted key stream yields one boundary.
scout.notes: Default 4 KiB boundaries mean small ASCII-key tests may not split; split/root-split tests should either generate boundary-producing keys or enough keys to hit `BoundaryDetector::default()`. Be explicit in tests so a missing split is not mistaken for broken path rewriting.

files_changed.note: Replaced the CORE-002 mutation placeholder with insert and shared immutable rewrite/split helpers.

dev.how: Implemented `insert` as a one-mutation call into the shared recursive rewrite engine. The engine loads only affected branches, applies sorted-key insert/replace semantics in the target leaf, splits leaf and internal replacement streams using `BoundaryDetector::default()`, stores freshly constructed nodes, propagates first-key separators upward, reuses unchanged sibling hashes, and returns a single new root hash.
dev.deviation: Instead of a separate explicit path-frame implementation for insert, I made `batch_mutate` the primary recursive rewrite engine and used `insert` as a thin one-item wrapper. This matches the R8 scouted optimisation approach while preserving the R4 path-to-root rewrite, content-addressed immutability, split propagation, and unchanged-sibling reuse semantics.

checklist.note: Put/insert inserts or replaces a sorted key/value entry, applies deterministic boundary splitting, and rewrites modified ancestors.
Splitting depends on the final sorted key stream and node hashes are content-addressed, so equal key/value sets produce equal roots in smoke checks regardless of insertion order.
Unmodified child references are copied unchanged into rewritten parents.

stories.note: The mutation API provides transparent key/value put semantics at the tree layer.
The shared rewrite machinery supports atomic multi-write root production used by batch mutation.

acceptance.criterion: insert(store, root_hash, key, value) returns a new root Hash distinct from the old one
acceptance.evidence: `insert` returns `Result<Hash, TreeError>` via `batch_mutate` at crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:22-30. Test asserts distinct roots at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:239-242.

acceptance.criterion: A Cursor on the new root can get(key) and returns the inserted value
acceptance.evidence: Hardening test creates a Cursor on the inserted/split root and asserts the inserted successor value at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:244-248.

acceptance.criterion: Inserting the same key twice returns a new root where get(key) returns the second value
acceptance.evidence: Leaf mutation replacement path updates existing values at crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:114-125; test asserts a distinct replacement root and second value lookup at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:249-254.

acceptance.criterion: When a leaf split occurs, both resulting leaves are stored in the store under their new hashes
acceptance.evidence: Leaf replacements split and store leaves at crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:200-212 and mutate.rs:258-265. Test forces a boundary split and asserts each root child hash resolves to a Leaf at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:256-262.

acceptance.criterion: When a root split occurs, the returned hash points to a new InternalNode with exactly two children
acceptance.evidence: Fixed root finalization now routes multi-child replacements through `store_root_internal`/`root_children`, which returns exactly two grouped child refs when needed, at crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:287-328. Tests assert two children for both simple and multi-boundary root splits at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:256-259 and 295-304.

acceptance.criterion: Unmodified sibling subtrees appear in the new InternalNode with their original child hashes unchanged
acceptance.evidence: Unchanged groups copy original child hashes in `rewrite_internal` at crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:162-168; test confirms the right sibling hash remains present after modifying the left subtree at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:280-293.

acceptance.criterion: Inserting keys in different orders into an empty tree produces the same root hash (history-independence, CN5, C17)
acceptance.evidence: Hardened tests assert reverse-order equality for boundary-induced split roots at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:264-278, multi-boundary split roots at lines 295-329, and 100 non-boundary keys at lines 331-343.

issues: Pre-harden implementation finalized root splits by storing `InternalNode::new(replacements)` directly, so a root split with more than two replacement children could violate the exact-two-child acceptance criterion and produce history-dependent roots. The new multi-boundary hardening test initially failed at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:316 before the fix.
fixes: Replaced direct multi-child root finalization with deterministic `store_root_internal`/`root_children` canonicalization at crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:287-328.
Added flattening and subtree reuse helpers at crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:331-383 so canonical root grouping is history-independent while preserving matching existing child subtrees.
Added hardening coverage for simple split, multi-boundary split, unchanged sibling reuse, replacement insert, and insertion-order determinism in crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs.

requirements.title: Delete with node merging and path-to-root rewrite
requirements.spec: Define a delete(store, root_hash, key) function in the mutate module. WHEN delete is called and the key exists, THE SYSTEM SHALL remove the entry from the target leaf and rewrite the path to root with new hashes. IF the resulting leaf is empty and is not the root, THE SYSTEM SHALL remove the leaf and its separator from the parent internal node. IF the resulting internal node has only one child after separator removal, THE SYSTEM SHALL collapse it by promoting the single child to replace the parent. THE SYSTEM SHALL return the new root Hash. WHEN delete is called and the key does not exist, THE SYSTEM SHALL return the original root hash unchanged — delete is idempotent. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT modify or delete nodes already stored in the store — only new nodes are written (P1). THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT merge nodes purely based on size; merging is triggered only by emptiness, not by a minimum-fill threshold.
acceptance: delete(store, root_hash, existing_key) returns a new root Hash distinct from the old one
A Cursor on the new root returns Ok(None) for the deleted key
delete(store, root_hash, absent_key) returns the original root hash unchanged
Deleting the last entry from a leaf removes the leaf's separator from the parent
Deleting all entries from a single-leaf tree returns the hash of an empty LeafNode
delete followed by insert of the same key produces a root hash equal to a fresh insert into a tree without the delete

files: crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:1-1 — add `delete` alongside insert and reuse path-rewrite helpers.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:62-75 — leaf entry removal must preserve sorted unique entries through `LeafNode::new`.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:107-120 — parent separator removal/collapse logic operates on internal child vectors.
crates/haematite/src/store/memory.rs:6-10 — old nodes cannot be deleted through the store API; only new nodes are written.
docs/design/core/briefs/CORE-002.json:93-103 — delete acceptance distinguishes absent-key idempotence, empty-root handling, and one-child internal collapse.
context: Absent-key delete is idempotent: return `Ok(root_hash)` immediately and do not write a replacement leaf or parent path.

If a non-root leaf becomes empty, bubble “no replacement child” to the parent so the parent removes that separator/child entry. If the root leaf becomes empty, store and return the hash of an empty `LeafNode`.
Do not merge nodes based on size or minimum fill. The brief only wants deletion-triggered emptiness removal and single-child internal collapse.
Deleting the first key in a non-empty child changes that child’s separator; parent rewrite must update it even when no node is removed.
scout.approach: Use the same traversal/path-frame mechanism as insertion. On the target leaf, binary-search for the key; if missing, return the original root hash unchanged. If present, remove the entry, then either store a new non-empty leaf and bubble its first-key separator/hash, bubble an empty replacement for non-root deletion, or store an empty root leaf for the single-leaf root case. While unwinding, remove empty child replacements, collapse internal nodes with one child by promoting that child hash/ref, otherwise rebuild and store a new internal node; finally return the promoted child hash or newly stored root hash.
scout.notes: Do not call any hypothetical `remove` on `MemoryStore` or try to mutate serialized bytes in place. Delete followed by insert hash equality depends on canonical empty-tree handling and absence of tombstone state.

files_changed.note: Added delete semantics and collapse/empty-child propagation to the shared rewrite engine.

dev.how: Implemented `delete` as a one-mutation call into the shared rewrite engine. Existing keys are removed from leaves and rewritten upward; absent keys return `Ok(root_hash)` without writing; empty non-root leaves bubble no replacement to parents; one-child internal nodes collapse by promoting their child; deleting the last root-leaf entry stores and returns the canonical empty leaf hash.
dev.deviation: Like insert, delete is implemented through the shared `batch_mutate` recursive rewrite engine rather than a separate path-frame stack. This preserves idempotent absent deletes, emptiness-only merging, single-child internal collapse, and immutable node storage while avoiding duplicated mutation code.

checklist.note: Delete removes existing keys, rewrites modified ancestors, removes empty child replacements, and collapses one-child internals.
Old nodes are never mutated or deleted and unaffected sibling hashes remain unchanged.

stories.note: The tree layer now supports transparent key/value delete semantics.

requirements.title: TreeError error type
requirements.spec: Define a TreeError enum in cursor.rs covering at minimum TreeError::MissingNode { hash: Hash } for store lookup failures and TreeError::InvalidNode for deserialisation failures. THE SYSTEM SHALL implement std::error::Error and std::fmt::Display for TreeError. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT use .unwrap() or .expect() in cursor.rs or mutate.rs — all fallible operations must propagate errors via Result<T, TreeError>. Both insert and delete must also return Result<Hash, TreeError>.
acceptance: TreeError implements std::error::Error
TreeError implements std::fmt::Display with a human-readable message naming the hash for MissingNode
Cursor::get returns Result<Option<Value>, TreeError>
insert returns Result<Hash, TreeError>
delete returns Result<Hash, TreeError>
grep -r 'unwrap()\|expect(' crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs finds no matches (CN2)

files: crates/haematite/src/tree/cursor.rs:1-1 — define public `TreeError` here so cursor and mutate can share it.
crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:1-1 — `insert`, `delete`, and `batch_mutate` should return `Result<Hash, TreeError>`.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:35-60 — `NodeError` already implements `Display` and `std::error::Error`; map constructor/structural failures into `TreeError::InvalidNode`.
crates/haematite/src/store/memory.rs:6-10 — store lookups expose missing nodes as `Option::None`; cursor/mutate should convert that to `MissingNode { hash }`.
crates/haematite/Cargo.toml:18-24 — lint settings enforce the no-unwrap/no-expect/no-panic policy beyond the brief’s grep check.
context: Keep `TreeError` in `cursor.rs` per the brief and re-export it from `tree/mod.rs`; `mutate.rs` can import it with `use super::cursor::TreeError` or `use super::TreeError` after wiring.

For compatibility with likely acceptance tests, include at least `TreeError::MissingNode { hash: Hash }` and a unit-like `TreeError::InvalidNode`; `From<NodeError> for TreeError` can map to `InvalidNode`.
`Display` for `MissingNode` should include the hash using `Hash`’s hex `Display`, satisfying the “naming the hash” requirement.
Do not hide fallible operations behind `unwrap_or` defaults that change behavior; propagate `Result` and treat invalid empty internal nodes or invalid constructor output as errors.
scout.approach: Define `#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)] pub enum TreeError { MissingNode { hash: Hash }, InvalidNode }`, implement `fmt::Display` with a human-readable message for both variants, implement `std::error::Error`, and implement `From<NodeError>` if it simplifies mutation helpers. Add a small `load_node<S: NodeStore + ?Sized>(store: &S, hash: Hash) -> Result<Node, TreeError>` helper in `cursor.rs` or duplicate a private equivalent in `mutate.rs` if visibility would otherwise leak. Ensure public signatures are `Cursor::get(&self, key: &[u8]) -> Result<Option<Vec<u8>>, TreeError>`, `insert(...) -> Result<Hash, TreeError>`, `delete(...) -> Result<Hash, TreeError>`, and `batch_mutate(...) -> Result<Hash, TreeError>`.
scout.notes: Because `NodeStore::get` already deserializes internally and returns `Option<Node>`, a corrupt serialized node in `MemoryStore` currently appears as `None`; do not over-engineer disk-store error handling in CORE-002. The required `InvalidNode` variant is still useful for malformed tree structure and `NodeError` from rebuilding nodes.

files_changed.note: Defined public `TreeError` and shared traversal error helpers.
All public mutation functions return `Result<Hash, TreeError>` and propagate structural errors without unwrap/expect.

dev.how: Added `#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)] pub enum TreeError { MissingNode { hash }, InvalidNode }`, implemented human-readable `Display`, `std::error::Error`, and `From<NodeError>`. Cursor and mutation code use `Result` throughout; explicit grep over `cursor.rs` and `mutate.rs` found no `unwrap()` or `expect(` matches.

THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT add any logic to tree/mod.rs beyond pub mod and pub use — all logic resides in the leaf modules (CN3). Update lib.rs to re-export Cursor, TreeError, insert, delete, and batch_mutate from the crate root.

crates/haematite/src/lib.rs:1-17 — crate root currently re-exports CORE-001 tree/store types; extend this list with cursor/mutate APIs.

docs/design/core/design.json:54-58 — CN1-CN4 include file-size, no-panics, module-root discipline, and public `Debug` requirements.
context: `tree/mod.rs` already follows CN3: it contains only `pub mod` declarations and `pub use` lines. Preserve that shape exactly.
`lib.rs` is allowed to declare modules and re-export public API items; keep wiring narrow and do not add helper logic there.
Rustfmt may reorder module declarations or grouped `pub use` items; that is fine as long as no `fn`, `struct`, `enum`, or `impl` appears in a `mod.rs`.
`haematite::Cursor` should resolve as a generic public type; consumers can name it with inferred type parameters from constructors.
scout.approach: After implementing the leaf modules, update `tree/mod.rs` with `pub use cursor::{Cursor, TreeError};` and `pub use mutate::{batch_mutate, delete, insert};` alongside the existing boundary/node exports. Update `lib.rs` by extending the existing `pub use tree::{...};` list to include `Cursor`, `TreeError`, `insert`, `delete`, and `batch_mutate`. Run `cargo check -p haematite` after wiring to catch signature/export visibility issues.
scout.notes: Do not move `NodeStore`, `TreeError`, or mutation helpers into `tree/mod.rs` to make imports easier; CN3 explicitly forbids module-root logic.

requirements.title: Batch mutation with single path-to-root rewrite
requirements.spec: Define a batch_mutate(store, root_hash, mutations) function in the mutate module where mutations is a sorted slice of (key, Option<value>) pairs — Some(value) for puts, None for deletes. WHEN batch_mutate is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL walk the tree once, applying all mutations to their target leaves in key order, splitting and merging nodes as needed, and rewriting the path to root exactly once. THE SYSTEM SHALL return the new root Hash. THE SYSTEM SHALL produce the same root hash as applying the same mutations individually via insert/delete calls — batch_mutate is an optimisation, not a different operation. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT rewrite the path to root more than once per batch_mutate call (CN8, C25). IF mutations is empty, THE SYSTEM SHALL return the original root hash unchanged.
acceptance: batch_mutate(store, root, [(k1, Some(v1)), (k2, Some(v2))]) returns a root hash equal to insert(insert(root, k1, v1), k2, v2)
batch_mutate(store, root, [(k1, None)]) returns a root hash equal to delete(root, k1)
batch_mutate with 100 mutations produces one root hash — not 100 intermediate roots
batch_mutate with an empty slice returns the original root hash unchanged
batch_mutate with interleaved puts and deletes produces the same result as the equivalent individual operations
A store instrumented with a write counter confirms that batch_mutate writes fewer nodes than the equivalent sequence of individual insert/delete calls

files: crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:1-1 — implement `batch_mutate` as the core multi-mutation rewrite engine, not a loop over `insert`/`delete`.
crates/haematite/src/tree/boundary.rs:1-49 — reuse deterministic boundary detection for splitting modified leaf/internal nodes during a batch.
crates/haematite/src/tree/node.rs:62-150 — batch rewriting must rebuild valid `LeafNode` and `InternalNode` values from sorted unique entries/children.
crates/haematite/src/store/memory.rs:6-10 — an instrumented `NodeStore` wrapper can count `put` calls to prove batch writes fewer nodes than individual mutation sequences.
docs/design/core/briefs/CORE-002.json:153-163 — batch acceptance requires equality with individual operations, empty-slice no-op, interleaved puts/deletes, and fewer writes.
context: The input mutations are specified as a sorted slice. If duplicate keys appear adjacently, batch semantics should match applying the slice in order; coalesce to the last effective operation per key before rebuilding each leaf.

`batch_mutate` must return exactly one final root hash and no intermediate public roots. Calling `insert`/`delete` repeatedly would satisfy equality but violate CN8/C25 and the write-count acceptance.

A single recursive walk over affected branches preserves unmodified sibling hashes naturally: partition sorted mutations by internal child, rewrite only children with non-empty mutation groups, and carry unchanged `(separator, hash)` pairs through untouched.
An empty mutation slice should return `Ok(root_hash)` without reading or writing the store.
scout.approach: Make `batch_mutate<S: NodeStore>(store: &mut S, root_hash: Hash, mutations: &[(Vec<u8>, Option<Vec<u8>>)]) -> Result<Hash, TreeError>` the primary implementation, with `insert` and `delete` thin one-item wrappers. Recursively rewrite nodes: load the current node once, apply all relevant mutations if it is a leaf, or partition the sorted mutation slice among internal children using the same separator selection as `Cursor::get`; unchanged children keep their original hashes, changed children return replacement child refs or deletion/collapse signals. After applying all changes under a node, split modified leaves/internal nodes by deterministic boundaries, collapse single-child internals where delete requires it, store each new node once, and return only the final root hash to the caller.
scout.notes: `mutate.rs` can become large because batch, insert, delete, split, collapse, and tests all land there. If it approaches CN1’s 500-line limit, keep the public `mutate.rs` facade but extract private helpers into submodules under `tree/mutate/` declared with `mod helper;` from `mutate.rs`; do not put logic in any `mod.rs`.

files_changed.note: Implemented `batch_mutate` as the primary sorted multi-mutation rewrite engine.

dev.how: Implemented `batch_mutate(store, root_hash, mutations)` as a single recursive walk over affected branches. It returns the original root for empty input, coalesces adjacent duplicate sorted keys to their last operation, partitions sorted mutations by internal child, rewrites only changed children, carries unchanged children through by hash, handles leaf splits and empty-child removal, and returns one final root hash without exposing intermediate roots.

checklist.note: Batch mutation produces one final root hash and rewrites each affected path once through the recursive partitioned rewrite engine rather than looping over public insert/delete calls.

stories.note: Multiple puts/deletes can be committed as one atomic tree-root update with results matching equivalent individual operations in smoke checks.

Test applies 100 mutations in one batch and uses one returned `batch` hash at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:391-412.

issues: The shared root-finalization bug also affected batch-generated multi-boundary roots: before hardening, batch/root finalization could preserve a non-canonical multi-child root shape rather than the deterministic two-child root shape needed for history-independent batch equivalence.
fixes: Fixed shared root finalization in crates/haematite/src/tree/mutate.rs:287-328 and subtree flatten/reuse in mutate.rs:331-383.
Added batch equivalence and write-count coverage, including a multi-boundary batch-vs-individual root assertion at crates/haematite/tests/core002.rs:318-329 and broad batch tests at lines 391-444.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement disk storage, compression, or on-disk node persistence — that is CORE-004
SHALL NOT implement WAL buffer, durable writes, or batch commit — those are CORE-005
SHALL NOT implement the structural tree diff between two root hashes — that is CORE-003
SHALL NOT implement shard actors, shard routing, or message dispatch — those are CORE-007 and CORE-008
SHALL NOT implement branching, merging, or fork operations — those are covered by the branching cluster
SHALL NOT produce more than one root hash per insert or delete call — batch commit is the WAL's responsibility (CN8), not the tree mutation layer's
